alert_title: payment-service /charge p99 latency above SLO, intermittent 500s

timeline.event: Deploy: payment-service v5.3.0 (changelog: tuned outbound HTTP client connection pool sizing for charge-service calls; structured logging for charge events)
Deploy: auth-service v2.9.0 (changelog: integrate fraud-score-service risk check into token validation flow)
Alert fired: payment-service /charge p99 latency above SLO

logs_excerpt: redis: INFO charge-cache hit rate at 90% (typically 97%)
payment-service: WARN /charge request queued 1200ms waiting for worker thread
postgres: INFO query latency nominal, no entries in slow query log
payment-service: WARN call to auth-service for token validation took 1840ms (typically under 100ms)
payment-service: INFO outbound connection pool to fraud-related endpoints: 0 idle, 40/40 in use
payment-service: INFO outbound connection pool to all other endpoints: 35/40 idle
auth-service: WARN fraud-score-service call took 1700ms

candidate_causes.label: auth-service v2.9.0 integrated a synchronous fraud-score-service risk check into the token validation flow, adding ~1700ms per call; payment-service's outbound pool to fraud-related endpoints is fully saturated while its pool to all other endpoints stays mostly idle, isolating the bottleneck to exactly the auth-service-dependent calls
candidate_causes.mechanism: The fraud-score-service lookup added to auth-service's validate_token path adds roughly 1700ms per call (auth-service's own p99 jumped from a 95ms baseline to 1840ms). Critically, payment-service's outbound connection pool is segmented by endpoint class: the pool serving fraud-related (auth) endpoints is at 100% utilization while the pool serving every other endpoint sits at only 12%. A general capacity or pool-sizing problem would show exhaustion across both pools; this asymmetry means only the auth-service-dependent path is starved, pointing directly at auth-service's new lookup as the cause rather than anything in payment-service itself.

candidate_causes.label: payment-service's own v5.3.0 deploy retuned outbound connection pool sizing, and that retuning is what caused the worker pool exhaustion
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's a deploy to the symptomatic service itself, shipped shortly before the alert, and its changelog explicitly mentions changing connection pool sizing -- the exact component implicated in the symptom, not just a generic recent-deploy coincidence.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by the pool-usage asymmetry: if the v5.3.0 retuning had misconfigured pooling broadly, the 'other endpoints' pool would show the same exhaustion as the fraud-endpoints pool. It doesn't (12% vs 100% usage), so the retuning itself isn't the bottleneck; only the specific downstream dependency (auth-service) is.

candidate_causes.label: Degraded Redis cache performance is slowing down charge processing
candidate_causes.why_tempting: redis_cache_hit_rate_pct genuinely dropped from a 97% baseline to 90% concurrent with the incident -- a real, measurable anomaly, not an obviously-nominal stat to dismiss.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because the hit-rate dip is a downstream effect, not a trigger: timed-out and retried charge requests recompute pricing instead of reading from a warm cache, producing exactly this kind of secondary hit-rate drop. postgres_query_latency_p50_ms staying nominal at 13ms confirms the database Redis would otherwise be protecting was never under real pressure, meaning the cache dip doesn't reflect genuine load needing a cache assist -- it's a symptom of the same incident, not its cause.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Make the fraud-score-service lookup in auth-service's validate_token path asynchronous or cached, or wrap it in a strict timeout + circuit breaker -- do not revert payment-service's v5.3.0 connection pool retuning (it isn't the cause) and do not scale Redis (its hit-rate dip is a symptom, not the trigger).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 2: both distractors now share literal evidence overlap with the true cause rather than being trivially nominal -- the deploy distractor explicitly touches 'connection pool sizing' (the same component implicated in the symptom), and the Redis distractor has a real, measured anomaly (97%->90%) rather than an obviously-healthy stat. Discrimination requires noticing the pool-usage asymmetry between endpoint classes (ruling out a pool-wide misconfiguration) and recognizing the cache-hit dip as a downstream effect of retried requests rather than a cause -- both require reasoning about evidence direction, not just presence/absence of an anomaly.

alert_title: cart-service checkout error rate above 5%, intermittent timeouts on add-to-cart

timeline.event: Deploy: cart-service v4.2.0 (changelog: localized UI copy strings; minor adjustment to client-side retry/backoff timing for localization-fallback API calls)
[external status feed] OktaCorp (identity provider): investigating elevated latency on token-introspection API
auth-service p99 latency on external identity-provider calls climbs to 2200ms (baseline 80ms)
payment-service request queue depth rising
Alert fired: cart-service checkout error rate above 5%

logs_excerpt: cart-service: ERROR add-to-cart request failed after retry exhaustion (14 occurrences in 5m)
cart-service: INFO redis-cache GET for cart-session averaging 6ms (typically 4ms)
cart-service: WARN payment pre-check call to payment-service timed out after 3000ms (32 occurrences in 5m)
payment-service: WARN worker pool queue depth climbing, 91% utilization
auth-service: INFO CPU and memory within normal range
payment-service: WARN call to auth-service exceeded 2000ms on 58 of last 100 requests
auth-service: WARN identity-provider introspection call latency elevated, p99 2200ms (typically 80ms)

candidate_causes.label: OktaCorp's token-introspection endpoint degraded (per its own status feed, timestamp-correlated with auth-service's latency climb), increasing auth-service's validation latency; that queued payment-service's calls to auth-service and exhausted its worker pool, cascading into cart-service checkout timeouts two hops upstream
candidate_causes.mechanism: auth-service's own CPU and memory are normal, ruling out a local resource problem; its latency spike to external identity-provider calls specifically (2200ms vs an 80ms baseline) lines up in time with OktaCorp's own status-feed report of introspection-endpoint degradation, meaning the slowdown is external. Because payment-service holds a worker thread for the duration of each auth-service call, that latency consumed payment-service's pool (91% utilization), and because cart-service's checkout flow makes a synchronous payment pre-check call to payment-service, the exhaustion propagated two hops upstream into cart-service's own timeouts -- without any code or infrastructure change inside cart-service itself.

candidate_causes.label: cart-service's v4.2.0 deploy, which adjusted client-side retry/backoff timing, introduced the checkout regression
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's the most recent deploy to the symptomatic service itself, and it explicitly touches retry/backoff timing -- the same kind of timeout/retry behavior visible in the symptom, not just an unrelated cosmetic change.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by the changelog specifying the retry/backoff change applies only to localization-fallback API calls, a separate code path from the payment pre-check that's actually timing out, and by cart_service_cpu_pct remaining a normal 38%.

candidate_causes.label: cart-service's Redis cache is degraded, slowing down checkout
candidate_causes.why_tempting: cart_service_redis_get_latency_ms genuinely rose from a 4ms baseline to 6ms concurrent with the incident -- a real, measured deviation, not an obviously-nominal stat, and Redis is cart-service's own direct, visible dependency.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because a 4ms->6ms shift is far too small to explain 3000ms timeouts, and cart_service_redis_hit_rate_pct stays healthy at 94%; the mild latency uptick is consistent with cart-service's own threads being backed up waiting on the payment pre-check (a downstream effect of the real bottleneck), not a degraded cache.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Add a timeout + circuit breaker (with a fail-open or cached fallback) around auth-service's calls to the identity provider's introspection endpoint so a third-party degradation can't block payment-service and cart-service; do not revert cart-service v4.2.0 (its retry change is on an unrelated code path) and do not scale cart-service's Redis (a 4ms->6ms shift isn't the bottleneck).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 3: true cause is two hops upstream (cart-service -> payment-service -> auth-service -> external identity provider) and the external trigger is no longer stated as a cause inside any service's own logs -- it's a separate status-feed timeline event that must be timestamp-correlated with auth-service's latency climb, not read off a single line. Both distractors now carry genuine, measured (if small) anomalies sharing literal vocabulary with the true cause (retry/backoff timing; latency uptick) rather than being trivially nominal, forcing the responder to judge magnitude and direction (cause vs. downstream effect) rather than just presence of an anomaly.

alert_title: order-service checkout-confirmation API showing degraded p95 latency and a rising rate of query-timeout errors

timeline.event: Deploy: order-service v6.1.0 (changelog: minor logging cleanup, no query changes)
Scheduled job started: tenant 'globalretail-co' nightly catalog-export batch job (reads large ranges from postgres)
Alert fired: order-service p95 latency above SLO, query timeout rate rising

logs_excerpt: order-service: WARN db query timeout after 4500ms on order-history lookup (typically <200ms)
postgres: INFO globalretail-co catalog-export job: 1.2M rows scanned, sequential scan on products table
order-service: WARN db connection pool at 92% utilization
postgres: INFO disk read IOPS at 94% of provisioned capacity (typically 30-40%)
order-service: INFO request rate steady at 140 req/s (baseline 130-150 req/s)

candidate_causes.label: globalretail-co's nightly catalog-export batch job is running a full sequential scan against postgres concurrently with normal traffic, consuming disk I/O and starving order-service's own queries
candidate_causes.mechanism: The catalog-export job is doing a 1.2M-row sequential scan, pushing disk IOPS to 94 percent of capacity (vs a 30-40 percent baseline) while order-service's own request rate stays flat at baseline. The query timeouts and pool exhaustion are a downstream effect of disk contention from a different tenant's batch workload, not a problem with order-service's own code, deploy, or traffic.

candidate_causes.label: order-service's own v6.1.0 deploy, shipped not long before the alert, introduced the regression
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's a deploy to the symptomatic service itself, shipped shortly before the alert -- the classic proximate-cause trap.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by the changelog stating logging-only changes, and by order_service_cpu_pct and request rate both staying at normal baseline levels; nothing in order-service's own workload or code changed.

candidate_causes.label: order-service's database connection pool is undersized for current load and needs to be scaled up
candidate_causes.why_tempting: order_service_db_pool_usage_pct is genuinely at 92 percent, a real and alarming-looking number, and scaling the pool is the standard first fix for this exact symptom shape.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because the pool isn't being exhausted by query volume (request rate is flat at baseline); it's exhausted because each query is taking far longer than normal (4500ms vs <200ms) due to I/O contention. Scaling the pool would let more slow queries queue up against the same contended disk, not fix the underlying problem.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Move or throttle the catalog-export batch job (read replica, off-peak scheduling, or IOPS throttling) so it stops contending with order-service's transactional queries for disk I/O -- do not roll back order-service v6.1.0 (unrelated) or scale order-service's connection pool (it would not address the I/O contention).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 2: the pool-usage distractor is the single most tempting wrong answer for this symptom shape (high pool usage -> scale the pool) and requires recognizing that query duration, not query volume, is driving the exhaustion. True cause is a multi-tenant noisy-neighbor pattern rather than anything in order-service's own code or capacity.

alert_title: recommendation-service /recommendations endpoint p99 latency climbing, ml-model-service call volume up sharply

timeline.event: Deploy: recommendation-service v3.0.0 (changelog: adds personalized-reranking code path behind feature flag personalized_rerank_v2, disabled by default)
Feature flag personalized_rerank_v2 enabled for 100% of traffic via config service (no code deploy)
Alert fired: recommendation-service p99 latency above SLO

logs_excerpt: recommendation-service: WARN /recommendations request made 18 calls to ml-model-service (typically 1 call per request)
ml-model-service: INFO request rate up 16x in the last 15 minutes
ml-model-service: WARN p99 inference latency at 340ms (typically 90ms) under elevated load
recommendation-service: INFO request rate steady at 95 req/s (baseline 90-100 req/s)
recommendation-service: INFO personalized_rerank_v2 flag at 100% rollout as of 15 minutes ago

candidate_causes.label: the personalized_rerank_v2 flag, flipped to 100% 15 minutes before the alert, added a per-item re-ranking call to ml-model-service for each recommended item instead of one batched call -- an N+1 pattern
candidate_causes.mechanism: The new code path makes one ml-model-service call per recommended item instead of a single batched call, multiplying recommendation-service's call volume roughly 18x per request (vs a baseline of 1) and pushing ml-model-service's own load and latency up as a downstream effect. The call-count-per-request metric is the direct fingerprint of an N+1 pattern, not a capacity problem.

candidate_causes.label: recommendation-service's v3.0.0 deploy, 2 hours prior, introduced the regression
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's the most recent code deploy to the symptomatic service, and it's the deploy that literally added the new code path -- a strong-looking proximate cause.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by timing: the deploy shipped 2 hours before the alert with the flag disabled, and the service ran normally that whole window; the alert started within minutes of the flag rollout, not the deploy, meaning the deploy alone with the flag off was inert.

candidate_causes.label: ml-model-service itself is degraded and needs scaling/investigation as the primary issue
candidate_causes.why_tempting: ml_model_service_p99_latency_ms genuinely tripled (90ms -> 340ms) and its CPU is elevated (71 percent) -- a real, measurable degradation, not a fabricated symptom.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because ml-model-service's latency increase is proportional to and caused by the 16x request-rate multiplier it's receiving, not an independent fault; nothing changed inside ml-model-service itself in this window.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Batch the personalized_rerank_v2 path into a single call per request instead of one call per item, or roll back the flag to 0% until fixed -- do not roll back recommendation-service v3.0.0 itself (the code path is inert until the flag is on) and do not scale ml-model-service (it would mask the N+1 pattern rather than fix it).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 2: tests whether the system attributes the cause to a feature-flag rollout event rather than the code deploy that shipped (inert) hours earlier, and whether it recognizes ml-model-service's real, measured degradation as a downstream consequence of call-volume multiplication rather than an independent root cause.

alert_title: notification-service order-confirmation delivery failing intermittently, error rate around 4%

timeline.event: Deploy: order-service v8.4.0 (changelog: adds optional gift_note field to order-confirmation event payload)
Deploy: notification-service v2.7.0 (changelog: dashboard UI text updates only)
Alert fired: notification-service order-confirmation delivery error rate above 3%

logs_excerpt: notification-service: ERROR failed to deserialize order-confirmation event: unexpected field gift_note (14 occurrences in 10m)
notification-service: INFO redis-cache hit rate at 93% (typically 95%)
notification-service: WARN message queue depth at 210 (typically under 50), retries accumulating
order-service: INFO order-confirmation events published normally, no errors
notification-service: INFO error rate isolated to orders containing a gift-wrap option (about 4% of order volume)

candidate_causes.label: order-service's v8.4.0 deploy added a new optional gift_note field to the order-confirmation event payload 3 hours before the alert; notification-service's deserializer doesn't recognize the field and rejects the event, only for orders that include a gift-wrap note
candidate_causes.mechanism: The failure is isolated to events containing the new field, and order-service confirms it's publishing successfully -- the mismatch is purely on the consuming side, three hours after the producing side changed its schema, explaining both the intermittency and the precise error rate matching gift-wrap order volume.

candidate_causes.label: notification-service's own v2.7.0 deploy, shipped closer in time to the alert, introduced the regression
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's the most recent deploy to the symptomatic service itself.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by the changelog stating UI text changes only, and by the failure being isolated specifically to gift-wrap orders rather than affecting the service broadly, which a generic deploy regression would not produce.

candidate_causes.label: notification-service's message queue is backlogged and needs scaling/draining
candidate_causes.why_tempting: notification_service_queue_depth is genuinely elevated (210 vs a baseline of 50) -- a real, measurable backlog, not a fabricated symptom.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because the backlog is retries of the same failing gift-wrap events piling up after repeated deserialization failures, not a primary capacity shortfall; clearing or scaling the queue would not stop the underlying rejections from recurring.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Update notification-service's event deserializer to tolerate (or explicitly handle) the new gift_note field, and coordinate schema changes between order-service and its consumers going forward -- do not roll back notification-service v2.7.0 (unrelated) and do not scale the message queue (the backlog is a symptom of the retries, not the cause).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 2: true cause is a schema change in an upstream producer (order-service) that happened hours earlier, surfacing as a consumer-side error in notification-service after an unrelated, more recent deploy to the symptomatic service itself -- tests whether the system looks past the more recent local deploy to find the actual breaking change further back in time and in a different service.

alert_title: order-service customers reporting missing order-confirmation emails for orders with a gift-wrap note, intermittent

timeline.event: Deploy: notification-service v2.9.0 (changelog: upgrade internal email-client library from v2 to v3 for the email-service integration)
Deploy: order-service v8.5.0 (changelog: minor copy changes to checkout confirmation page)
Alert fired: customer support tickets rising for missing confirmation emails on gift-wrapped orders

logs_excerpt: order-service: INFO order-confirmation event published successfully for all orders, no errors
notification-service: INFO event received and processed, forwarding to email-service
email-service: WARN send request rejected, HTTP 422 unrecognized field gift_note_format (9 occurrences in 30m)
notification-service: WARN retry queue depth elevated, 140 (typically under 40)
email-service: INFO no deploys in the last 14 days, service version unchanged
email-service: INFO error rate isolated to requests originating from notification-service's v3 email-client library (identified via client-version header)

candidate_causes.label: notification-service's v2.9.0 deploy upgraded its internal email-client library from v2 to v3, which serializes the optional gift-wrap note using a new field name email-service's deployed version doesn't recognize
candidate_causes.mechanism: email-service itself hasn't deployed anything in 14 days and explicitly attributes the errors to the v3 client header -- the mismatch is purely a version-skew issue between a library upgrade on one side and an unchanged service on the other, two hops away from where the symptom is visible (order-service), and only affects orders with a gift-wrap note since that's the only payload variant using the new field.

candidate_causes.label: order-service's own v8.5.0 deploy, much closer in time to the alert, caused the missing emails
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's the most recent deploy, and the symptom is visible from the order-service side (customers reporting it through order-service).
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by order-service's own publish success rate staying at 100% and the deploy being copy-only; the failure is occurring two services downstream, not in order-service.

candidate_causes.label: notification-service's retry queue is backed up and needs more capacity
candidate_causes.why_tempting: notification_service_retry_queue_depth is genuinely elevated (140 vs a baseline of 40), a real and visible anomaly in the service most directly in the middle of the chain.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because the backlog is retries of the same rejected gift-wrap requests accumulating after email-service's 422s, a downstream effect of the version mismatch, not an independent capacity problem; scaling the queue would not stop the rejections.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Update email-service to support the v3 client's gift_note_format field, or pin notification-service's email-client library to v2 until email-service is upgraded in lockstep -- do not roll back order-service v8.5.0 (unrelated) and do not scale notification-service's retry queue (the backlog is a symptom, not the cause).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 3: true cause is two hops upstream from where the symptom is reported (order-service -> notification-service -> email-service) and is a library/version-skew issue rather than a deploy, config flag, or third-party outage -- a different mechanism class from the other incidents, testing whether the system can trace an intermittent, narrowly-scoped failure back through two service hops to a dependency-version mismatch rather than stopping at the most recently deployed, locally-visible service.

alert_title: api-gateway p99 latency has been creeping upward over the last 3 hours, no error spike, requests still succeeding

timeline.event: Deploy: api-gateway v4.6.0 (changelog: refactors the auth-service client wrapper to support a new retry policy)
p99 latency begins a slow, steady climb (no alert threshold crossed yet)
Alert fired: api-gateway p99 latency above SLO (slow-burn, not a spike)

logs_excerpt: api-gateway: INFO request rate steady at 410 req/s (baseline 380-420 req/s)
api-gateway: WARN outbound connection pool to auth-service at 870/900 in use (typically under 200)
auth-service: INFO p99 latency at 110ms (typically 95ms)
api-gateway: INFO error rate at 0.1% (typically 0.1%), no error spike
api-gateway: INFO outbound connection pool to auth-service has grown steadily for the last 3 hours, no corresponding drop in idle connections elsewhere

candidate_causes.label: api-gateway's v4.6.0 deploy, which refactored the auth-service client wrapper for a new retry policy, introduced a connection leak -- retried connections aren't released back to the pool
candidate_causes.mechanism: Pool usage growth started at the same time as (and only as) the v4.6.0 deploy, traffic is flat, and there's no error spike -- the signature of a slow resource leak introduced by that specific deploy, not an external dependency problem. The leak is local to api-gateway's own connection management.

candidate_causes.label: auth-service itself is degraded and is the upstream cause of api-gateway's latency creep
candidate_causes.why_tempting: auth_service_p99_latency_ms is genuinely elevated (95ms -> 110ms), and in similar incidents the real cause has been one hop upstream in exactly this kind of dependency -- blaming the upstream service by pattern alone is a strong-looking move.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by magnitude and direction: auth-service's own latency increase (15ms) is far too small to explain api-gateway's SLO-violating creep, and is more plausibly a downstream effect of api-gateway holding far more connections open against it (870/900) than a cause of api-gateway's problem.

candidate_causes.label: organic traffic growth is pushing api-gateway past its capacity
candidate_causes.why_tempting: Latency creeping upward over hours looks exactly like what gradual capacity exhaustion from traffic growth would produce.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because api_gateway_request_rate_per_s has stayed flat (380-420 req/s) for the whole window; there's no growth in the thing that would need to grow for this explanation to hold.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Fix the connection leak in api-gateway's v4.6.0 auth-service client wrapper (ensure retried connections are released back to the pool) or roll back that specific deploy -- do not investigate auth-service as the primary cause (its latency increase is a downstream effect) and do not provision for traffic growth (volume is flat).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 2, deliberately inverted: every other incident in this set has the true cause one or more hops upstream of the symptomatic service. This one's true cause IS local to the symptomatic service itself, and the upstream service shows a real but secondary latency uptick that's tempting specifically because it matches the pattern other incidents trained toward. Tests whether the system over-generalizes check-upstream-first rather than evaluating each incident's evidence on its own, plus recognition of a silent SLO-drift pattern (no error spike) rather than a hard-failure signature.

alert_title: recommendation-service intermittently returning stale recommendation data immediately after a user's preferences are updated, no errors logged

timeline.event: Deploy: recommendation-service v3.1.0 (changelog: moves cache invalidation from a synchronous post-write step to an async background task for better write latency)
Customer reports rising about updated preferences still showing old recommendations
Alert fired: recommendation-service stale-read rate above threshold (detected via internal consistency check, not an error rate)

logs_excerpt: recommendation-service: INFO preference update written to postgres successfully (avg write latency 8ms, improved from 45ms pre-v3.1.0)
redis: INFO cache eviction rate at 12% (typically 3%), key churn elevated
recommendation-service: INFO cache-invalidation background task queue depth at 60 (typically under 10)
recommendation-service: INFO stale-read consistency check flagged 22 reads serving pre-update data within 2s of the corresponding write (typically 0)
ml-model-service: INFO model version v14.2, last updated 9 days ago, unchanged

candidate_causes.label: the v3.1.0 deploy moved cache invalidation from synchronous to an asynchronous background task; a read arriving in the window between the write completing and the background invalidation task running can still be served the old cached value
candidate_causes.mechanism: The timing signature (stale reads specifically within a short window after writes, correlated with the invalidation queue depth) plus the deploy's own changelog describing the exact ordering change is the fingerprint of a write-then-invalidate race, not a data or model freshness problem. The improved write latency (45ms -> 8ms) is the same change that introduced the race -- a net-positive-looking deploy metric masking the real defect.

candidate_causes.label: Redis itself is degraded (elevated eviction rate) and serving inconsistent data as a result
candidate_causes.why_tempting: redis_eviction_rate_pct genuinely quadrupled (3% -> 12%), a real and measurable anomaly directly involving the cache layer implicated in stale reads.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because the elevated eviction rate is itself a downstream effect of the same incident: the async invalidation task now runs as a backlog (queue depth 60 vs a baseline of 10), causing bursts of invalidation and repopulation that increase churn; eviction rate didn't cause the staleness, the architecture change causing the staleness also caused the elevated eviction rate.

candidate_causes.label: recommendation-service is serving stale results because ml-model-service is using an outdated model version
candidate_causes.why_tempting: Stale is the literal word used in both the symptom and this explanation, and model-freshness issues are a classic, plausible-sounding cause of outdated-looking recommendations.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because ml-model-service's model version (v14.2) hasn't changed in 9 days, well before this incident started, and the symptom is specifically about a user's own just-updated preferences not being reflected, not about model recommendations being generically out of date.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Make cache invalidation synchronous with the write again (accepting the latency cost), or add a short-lived negative-cache or read-through guard for the window between write and async invalidation -- do not focus remediation on Redis eviction tuning (a downstream symptom) or on ml-model-service's model freshness (unrelated).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 3: this is a data-correctness failure (silently wrong responses), not a latency or error-rate failure -- a different failure-mode class from every other incident in this set. The true cause is a race condition introduced by an ordering change in the same deploy that's primarily visible through an improvement metric (write latency got better), making it easy to read that deploy as purely positive. Both distractors share literal vocabulary with the symptom (stale) and have real, measurable (but secondary) anomalies, testing whether the system distinguishes a downstream consequence of the race from its actual cause.
